content.text: Querying at Scale

Resource Graph is designed to query across thousands of subscriptions simultaneously. The key levers are scope selection (management group vs. individual subscriptions), query optimization (filter by type before accessing properties), pagination via skipToken for result sets larger than 1000 rows, and rate-limit awareness (15 queries/second per tenant). This document covers each lever with production-grade patterns.

attrs.alt: Resource Graph querying at scale — scope hierarchy and pagination flow

content.text: The --management-groups flag is the single highest-leverage change for tenant-wide queries. Without it, az graph query silently scopes to your currently selected subscription and misses everything else.
Scope Selection: Management Groups vs Subscriptions

Resource Graph accepts scope as either a list of subscription IDs (--subscriptions) or one or more management group IDs (--management-groups). A management group scope recursively includes all subscriptions below it — including those that join the group after the query is written. This makes management group scope the right default for any query that must cover an entire organization.

# Querying across an entire management group (preferred for tenant-wide inventory)
az graph query \
  -q "Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines' | project name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, location" \
  --management-groups mg-corp \
  --output table

# Querying multiple specific subscriptions
az graph query \
  -q "Resources | where type !startswith 'microsoft.resources' | summarize Count = count() by subscriptionId" \
  --subscriptions sub-id-1 sub-id-2 sub-id-3 \
  --output table

# Without --management-groups or --subscriptions, the query runs against
# the currently selected subscription only (az account show --query id -o tsv)
Scope flag
Reaches
When to use
(none)
Currently selected subscription only
Ad-hoc local debugging; never for tenant-wide work
--subscriptions id1 id2 ...
Exactly the listed subscription IDs
Targeting a specific set of subscriptions; CI/CD per-sub jobs
--management-groups mg-id
All subs recursively under the MG
Organization-wide inventory and compliance sweeps
--management-groups tenant-root-id
Every subscription in the tenant
Tenant-level audits; slowest — use sparingly
Paginating Past the 1000-Row Limit

Resource Graph returns at most 1000 rows per API call regardless of total result count. The response body includes a skipToken field when more rows are available. Pass that token as --skip-token in the next CLI call (or as $skipToken in the REST body). Always pair pagination with order by so each page returns a deterministic slice of the result set.

# Full pagination loop — collects ALL matching resources across pages
# Resource Graph returns at most 1000 rows per call regardless of result count.

ALL_RESULTS='[]'
SKIP_TOKEN=''
PAGE=0

while true; do
  PAGE=$((PAGE + 1))

  if [[ -z "$SKIP_TOKEN" ]]; then
    RESULT=$(az graph query \
      -q "Resources
         | where type =~ 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines'
         | extend vmSize = tostring(properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize)
         | project name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, vmSize, location
         | order by subscriptionId asc, name asc" \
      --management-groups mg-corp \
      -o json)
  else
    RESULT=$(az graph query \
      -q "Resources
         | where type =~ 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines'
         | extend vmSize = tostring(properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize)
         | project name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, vmSize, location
         | order by subscriptionId asc, name asc" \
      --management-groups mg-corp \
      --skip-token "$SKIP_TOKEN" \
      -o json)
  fi

  BATCH=$(echo "$RESULT" | python3 -c 'import json,sys; print(json.dumps(json.load(sys.stdin)["data"]))')
  BATCH_SIZE=$(echo "$BATCH" | python3 -c 'import json,sys; print(len(json.load(sys.stdin)))')

  ALL_RESULTS=$(echo "$ALL_RESULTS $BATCH" | python3 -c \
    'import json,sys; parts=sys.stdin.read().split(None,1); print(json.dumps(json.loads(parts[0]) + json.loads(parts[1])))')

  SKIP_TOKEN=$(echo "$RESULT" | python3 -c \
    'import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin).get("skipToken",""))')

  echo "Page $PAGE: $BATCH_SIZE rows fetched"

  if [[ -z "$SKIP_TOKEN" || "$BATCH_SIZE" -lt 1000 ]]; then
    break
  fi
done

TOTAL=$(echo "$ALL_RESULTS" | python3 -c 'import json,sys; print(len(json.load(sys.stdin)))')
echo "Total VMs collected: $TOTAL"
echo "$ALL_RESULTS" > all-vms.json
Query Performance Patterns

Query execution time in Resource Graph is proportional to the number of rows that must be inspected before the type filter narrows the scan. The ARM index stores a B-tree on the type column, so placing | where type =~ '...' as the first operator after the table name turns a full-table scan into a targeted index lookup. Every other operator then operates only on matching resources.
Throttling and Retry Patterns

Resource Graph allows 15 queries per second per tenant. Automation that fires many queries in rapid succession — for example a CI/CD pipeline scanning dozens of subscriptions — will hit HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header. Always implement exponential backoff and cap parallel query fan-out to 5 concurrent requests. The bash pattern below shows both a retry wrapper and a semaphore for parallel subscription sweeps.

# Handling throttling and rate limits
# Resource Graph allows 15 queries/second per tenant.
# Under sustained load (batch processing, automation) you will hit 429 Too Many Requests.

# Bash: retry with exponential backoff on 429
run_graph_query() {
  local QUERY=$1
  local MAX_RETRIES=5
  local WAIT=2

  for attempt in $(seq 1 $MAX_RETRIES); do
    HTTP_STATUS=$(az graph query -q "$QUERY" --management-groups mg-corp \
      -o json 2>/tmp/graph_err; echo $?)

    # az graph query exits 0 on success, non-zero on error
    if [[ $HTTP_STATUS -eq 0 ]]; then
      return 0
    fi

    if grep -q 'ThrottlingException\|429' /tmp/graph_err; then
      echo "Throttled — waiting ${WAIT}s (attempt $attempt)"
      sleep $WAIT
      WAIT=$((WAIT * 2))
    else
      cat /tmp/graph_err
      return 1
    fi
  done
  echo 'ERROR: max retries exceeded'
  return 1
}

# When running many queries in parallel (e.g. per-subscription sweeps),
# use a semaphore to cap concurrent requests to 5 (the per-tenant parallel limit)
CONCURRENCY=5
PIDS=()

for SUB in $(az account list --query '[].id' -o tsv); do
  # Run at most $CONCURRENCY queries in parallel
  while [[ ${#PIDS[@]} -ge $CONCURRENCY ]]; do
    for i in "${!PIDS[@]}"; do
      kill -0 "${PIDS[$i]}" 2>/dev/null || unset 'PIDS[$i]'
    done
    PIDS=("${PIDS[@]}")
    sleep 0.2
  done

  az graph query \
    -q "Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines' | project name, resourceGroup" \
    --subscriptions $SUB -o json > result-$SUB.json &
  PIDS+=($!)
done
wait
echo 'All subscription queries complete'
Limit
Value
Notes
Max rows per call
Use skipToken for more; REST supports $top up to 1000
Throttle: queries/second
15 per tenant
Shared across all users and automation in the tenant
Throttle: parallel queries
5 per tenant
Exceeded requests queue; cap fan-out explicitly
Query timeout
30 seconds
Complex queries on tenants with millions of resources may hit this
Max subscriptions per call
Use management group scope instead when near this limit
Max skipToken hops
Unlimited
Iterate until skipToken is absent or batch size < 1000
Python SDK Pagination

The Python SDK (azure-mgmt-resourcegraph) exposes the same skipToken mechanism as the CLI but in an object model, making it easier to implement pagination loops without string manipulation of JSON responses. The QueryRequestOptions.top and .skip_token parameters map directly to the REST API $top and $skipToken fields. Use the async client (azure.mgmt.resourcegraph.aio) when running inside an asyncio event loop.

# Python SDK pagination for tenant-wide queries
# pip install azure-mgmt-resourcegraph azure-identity

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resourcegraph import ResourceGraphClient
from azure.mgmt.resourcegraph.models import (
    QueryRequest,
    QueryRequestOptions,
    ManagementGroupsQuery,
)

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ResourceGraphClient(credential)

QUERY = """
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines'
| extend
    vmSize    = tostring(properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize),
    osType    = tostring(properties.storageProfile.osDisk.osType),
    powerState = tostring(properties.extended.instanceView.powerState.code)
| project name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, vmSize, osType, powerState, location
| order by subscriptionId asc, name asc
"""

all_vms = []
skip_token = None

while True:
    options = QueryRequestOptions(top=1000, skip_token=skip_token)
    request = QueryRequest(
        management_groups=['mg-corp'],
        query=QUERY,
        options=options,
    )
    result = client.resources(request)
    all_vms.extend(result.data)
    print(f'Fetched {len(result.data)} rows  total so far: {len(all_vms)}')

    skip_token = result.skip_token
    if not skip_token or len(result.data) < 1000:
        break

print(f'Total VMs: {len(all_vms)}')

# Write to CSV
import csv
with open('vm-inventory.csv', 'w', newline='') as f:
    if all_vms:
        writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=all_vms[0].keys())
        writer.writeheader()
        writer.writerows(all_vms)
print('Exported vm-inventory.csv')
Parallel Per-Subscription Sweeps with asyncio

When you need subscription-level isolation of results — for example to generate one report file per subscription — use asyncio.gather with a semaphore to fan out queries while respecting the 5-parallel-query limit. This pattern completes in O(max_sub_time) rather than O(sum_of_sub_times) while staying within rate limits. Always handle exceptions from individual subscription queries so one failure does not abort the entire sweep.

# Parallel per-subscription sweep using Python asyncio
# Useful when you want subscription-level isolation of results
# while staying within the 5-parallel-query limit.

import asyncio
import json
from azure.identity.aio import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resourcegraph.aio import ResourceGraphClient
from azure.mgmt.resourcegraph.models import QueryRequest, QueryRequestOptions

QUERY = "Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/publicipaddresses' | project name, resourceGroup, location, subscriptionId"
SEMAPHORE = asyncio.Semaphore(5)   # respect 5-parallel-query limit

async def query_subscription(client, sub_id):
    async with SEMAPHORE:
        rows = []
        skip_token = None
        while True:
            opts = QueryRequestOptions(top=1000, skip_token=skip_token)
            req = QueryRequest(subscriptions=[sub_id], query=QUERY, options=opts)
            result = await client.resources(req)
            rows.extend(result.data)
            skip_token = result.skip_token
            if not skip_token or len(result.data) < 1000:
                break
        return rows

async def main():
    from azure.mgmt.subscription.aio import SubscriptionClient
    cred = DefaultAzureCredential()
    sub_client = SubscriptionClient(cred)
    sub_ids = [s.subscription_id async for s in sub_client.subscriptions.list()]
    print(f'Scanning {len(sub_ids)} subscriptions')

    graph_client = ResourceGraphClient(cred)
    tasks = [query_subscription(graph_client, sid) for sid in sub_ids]
    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)

    all_rows = []
    for sid, result in zip(sub_ids, results):
        if isinstance(result, Exception):
            print(f'ERROR {sid}: {result}')
        else:
            all_rows.extend(result)
    print(f'Total public IPs: {len(all_rows)}')
    return all_rows

asyncio.run(main())
Querying Change History at Scale

The resourcechanges table records Create, Update, and Delete events for ARM resources and is queryable with the same KQL operators as the Resources table. It supports tenant-wide scope via management group. Common patterns include: change volume by type over a rolling window, deletion audit trails by identity, and configuration drift detection by comparing changedBy against known service principals.

// Querying resource changes at scale using the resourcechanges table
// resourcechanges is only available in Resource Graph when Change Analysis is enabled.

// 1. Resources created or deleted in the last 7 days — tenant-wide
resourcechanges
| where properties.changeType in ('Create', 'Delete')
| extend
    changeType   = tostring(properties.changeType),
    resourceType = tostring(properties.targetResourceType),
    changedBy    = tostring(properties.changedBy),
    changeTime   = todatetime(properties.changeAttributes.timestamp)
| where changeTime > ago(7d)
| summarize Count = count() by changeType, resourceType
| order by Count desc

// 2. Who deleted resources (audit trail)
resourcechanges
| where properties.changeType =~ 'Delete'
| extend
    resourceName = tostring(properties.targetResourceId),
    resourceType = tostring(properties.targetResourceType),
    changedBy    = tostring(properties.changedBy),
    changeTime   = todatetime(properties.changeAttributes.timestamp)
| where changeTime > ago(24h)
| project resourceName, resourceType, changedBy, changeTime
| order by changeTime desc

// 3. Configuration drift — resources modified outside of expected IaC pipeline
// Correlate changedBy with known service principals
let KNOWN_PRINCIPALS = dynamic([
    'sp-terraform-prod@contoso.onmicrosoft.com',
    'sp-bicep-cicd@contoso.onmicrosoft.com'
]);
resourcechanges
| where properties.changeType =~ 'Update'
| extend
    changedBy  = tostring(properties.changedBy),
    changeTime = todatetime(properties.changeAttributes.timestamp)
| where changeTime > ago(7d)
| where changedBy !in (KNOWN_PRINCIPALS)
| project
    resourceId   = tostring(properties.targetResourceId),
    resourceType = tostring(properties.targetResourceType),
    changedBy,
    changeTime
| order by changeTime desc
Exporting Results to Blob Storage

For ongoing governance reporting, run paginated Resource Graph queries on a schedule and upload the JSON output to Azure Blob Storage. Downstream consumers — Power BI, Azure Data Explorer, Databricks — can read directly from blob storage without hitting Resource Graph API limits. Name blobs with date suffixes to build a time-series history of resource inventory changes.

# Export large Resource Graph results to Azure Blob Storage for downstream analysis

STORAGE_ACCOUNT='sagovernance01'
CONTAINER='graph-exports'
TODAY=$(date +%Y%m%d)

# Helper: paginate and write to file
paginate_to_file() {
  local QUERY=$1
  local OUTFILE=$2
  local ALL='[]'
  local SKIP_TOKEN=''
  local PAGE=0

  while true; do
    PAGE=$((PAGE+1))
    if [[ -z "$SKIP_TOKEN" ]]; then
      RESULT=$(az graph query -q "$QUERY" --management-groups mg-corp -o json)
    else
      RESULT=$(az graph query -q "$QUERY" --management-groups mg-corp --skip-token "$SKIP_TOKEN" -o json)
    fi
    BATCH=$(echo "$RESULT" | python3 -c 'import json,sys; print(json.dumps(json.load(sys.stdin)["data"]))')
    SKIP_TOKEN=$(echo "$RESULT" | python3 -c 'import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin).get("skipToken",""))')
    BATCH_SIZE=$(echo "$BATCH" | python3 -c 'import json,sys; print(len(json.load(sys.stdin)))')
    ALL=$(echo "$ALL $BATCH" | python3 -c \
      'import json,sys; p=sys.stdin.read().split(None,1); print(json.dumps(json.loads(p[0])+json.loads(p[1])))')
    echo "Page $PAGE: $BATCH_SIZE rows"
    [[ -z "$SKIP_TOKEN" || "$BATCH_SIZE" -lt 1000 ]] && break
  done

  echo "$ALL" > "$OUTFILE"
  echo "Written $(echo "$ALL" | python3 -c 'import json,sys; print(len(json.load(sys.stdin)))') rows to $OUTFILE"
}

# Export VM inventory
paginate_to_file \
  "Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines' | extend vmSize=tostring(properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize) | project name,resourceGroup,subscriptionId,vmSize,location" \
  /tmp/vms-$TODAY.json

# Export storage account inventory
paginate_to_file \
  "Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.storage/storageaccounts' | extend sku=tostring(sku.name), kind=tostring(kind) | project name,resourceGroup,subscriptionId,sku,kind,location" \
  /tmp/storage-$TODAY.json

# Upload both to blob storage
for FILE in /tmp/vms-$TODAY.json /tmp/storage-$TODAY.json; do
  BLOB_NAME="$(basename $FILE)"
  az storage blob upload \
    --account-name $STORAGE_ACCOUNT \
    --container-name $CONTAINER \
    --name $BLOB_NAME \
    --file $FILE \
    --auth-mode login \
    --overwrite
  echo "Uploaded $BLOB_NAME"
done
Scale Anti-Patterns to Avoid
Anti-pattern
Consequence
Correct approach
Querying without --management-groups in multi-sub environments
Silent scope miss — only the selected subscription is queried
Always pass --management-groups or explicit --subscriptions in automation
No order by before paginating with skipToken
Different rows on each page — duplicates and gaps in the full result set
Always include order by on a unique column when paginating
Running 20+ parallel queries without a semaphore
HTTP 429 storm; automation fails or produces incomplete data
Cap concurrency at 5 using a semaphore or xargs -P 5
Accessing properties before type filter
Full-table scan — times out on tenants with millions of resources
Place | where type =~ '...' as the first operator
Retrying 429 immediately without backoff
Hammers the throttle limit; ban window extends
Exponential backoff: 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s with jitter
select * via project-away instead of project
Large payloads; slow deserialization; wastes pagination quota
Always project only the columns downstream code actually uses
Summary

Effective large-scale querying with Resource Graph requires four habits: use --management-groups instead of --subscriptions for organization-wide scope, always filter by type =~ '...' as the first operator, implement a skipToken pagination loop when result counts may exceed 1000, and build exponential backoff into any automation that runs more than a few queries per second. With these patterns in place, Resource Graph can inventory millions of resources across thousands of subscriptions in seconds rather than hours.
